title: Object-Oriented Three-Tier-Plus Computing: Client-Server for Adults
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group's whitepaper examines the maturation of three-tier client-server computing in 1996, presenting the 'Three-Tier Plus' model that adds MPP/clustered SMP as a fourth tier for data warehousing. The study synthesizes field research from Aberdeen consulting assignments showing that organizations implementing 3-Tier architectures achieve four key business outcomes: improved enterprise efficiency, multi-source information access, scalability for emerging technologies, and superior ROI. Aberdeen argues object-oriented programming is critical for partitioning applications across tiers and predicts NT Server will close the gap with Unix on mid-tier within 24 months.
importance_rationale: One of the most comprehensive 1996 frameworks for enterprise client-server architecture; Aberdeen's 'Three-Tier Plus' model captured the evolution from 3-tier to n-tier computing that became the foundation for all modern enterprise application architecture.
relevance_rationale: The three-tier model (presentation, logic, data) remains the foundational pattern for web applications, microservices, and cloud-native architectures; Aberdeen's 1996 observations on application partitioning, OO programming, and MPP scaling are directly applicable to modern Kubernetes/container architectures.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen predicted NT Server would close the gap with Unix on mid-tier within 24 months (verified: NT 4.0 achieved this by 1997-1998); predicted MPP machines would achieve broader market acceptance (verified: data warehouse MPP systems became mainstream by 2000); mainframe residual value decline prediction fully confirmed.
